The **National Rugby League** (also known as the **NRL Telstra Premiership** for sponsorship reasons) is a professional [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") competition in [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania") which is currently contested by 17 teams from [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(state) "Victoria (state)"), the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory"), and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand").

Tracing its origins back to the [New South Wales Rugby League](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League "New South Wales Rugby League"), which formed in 1908, premier rugby league competition in Australia had gone through numerous iterations, most notably during the [Super League war](/wiki/Super_League_war "Super League war") in the 1990s,[[2]](#cite_note-2) which resulted in the NRL being formed in 1998 as a joint partnership between the [Australian Rugby League](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League_Commission "Australian Rugby League Commission") (ARL) and the [News Corporation](/wiki/News_Corporation "News Corporation")-controlled [Super League](/wiki/Super_League_(Australia) "Super League (Australia)").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The partnership was dissolved in 2012, with control of the NRL going to the re-constituted ARL, which was re-structured with an independent board of directors and renamed the [Australian Rugby League Commission](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League_Commission "Australian Rugby League Commission").

Each NRL season typically runs from March to October, with each team playing 24 matches. The first-placed team at the end of the regular season are awarded the [J. J. Giltinan Shield](/wiki/J._J._Giltinan_Shield "J. J. Giltinan Shield") for winning the [minor premiership](/wiki/Minor_premiership "Minor premiership"). This is followed by a [finals series](/wiki/NRL_finals_system "NRL finals system") contested between the top eight placed teams from the regular season, which determines the two teams to compete in the [NRL Grand Final](/wiki/NRL_Grand_Final "NRL Grand Final") held at [Stadium Australia](/wiki/Stadium_Australia "Stadium Australia"). The team that wins the match are awarded the [Provan-Summons Trophy](#Provan-Summons_trophy) in recognition for becoming the [premiers](#Title_winners) of the season,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and qualifies them to compete in the [World Club Challenge](/wiki/World_Club_Challenge "World Club Challenge") against the champions of the English [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The reigning premiers are the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos"), having won their seventh premiership in the [2025 NRL season](/wiki/2025_NRL_season "2025 NRL season").

As of 2023 the NRL is the richest rugby league football league and overall rugby league (including [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") and [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league")) in the world by revenue. It is also the [31st richest sports league](/wiki/List_of_professional_sports_leagues_by_revenue "List of professional sports leagues by revenue") in the world and the second richest in Australia behind the [Australian Football League](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the National Rugby League](/wiki/History_of_the_National_Rugby_League "History of the National Rugby League")

See also: [Rugby league in Australia](/wiki/Rugby_league_in_Australia "Rugby league in Australia") and [List of NRL records](/wiki/List_of_NRL_records "List of NRL records")

### Origin and establishment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origin and establishment")]

The [New South Wales Rugby League](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League "New South Wales Rugby League") ran the major rugby league competition of New South Wales from its [inception in 1908](/wiki/1908_NSWRFL_season "1908 NSWRFL season") until [1994](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League_season_1994 "New South Wales Rugby League season 1994"). Following the introduction of a new format for interstate rugby league, the [State of Origin series](/wiki/State_of_Origin_series "State of Origin series") in 1980, the decade of the 1980s brought about expansion of the [NSWRL premiership](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League_premiership "New South Wales Rugby League premiership"), with the introduction of commercial sponsorship, the [Winfield Cup](/wiki/Winfield_Cup "Winfield Cup"), and the addition of non-Sydney-based teams, [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders") and [Illawarra](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers") in 1982.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Although this move brought more interest in the competition statewide in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), it would spell the beginning of the demise of some of the traditional Sydney-based clubs as well as having a negative effect on the [Brisbane Rugby League premiership](/wiki/Brisbane_Rugby_League_premiership "Brisbane Rugby League premiership"). Following the 1983 season, Sydney foundation club [Newtown Jets](/wiki/Newtown_Jets "Newtown Jets") were ultimately forced to withdraw from the competition because of financial difficulties.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Further expansion of the league followed in [1988](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League_season_1988 "New South Wales Rugby League season 1988"), with another three teams based outside Sydney introduced to the competition; the [Newcastle Knights](/wiki/Newcastle_Knights "Newcastle Knights") and the first two Queensland teams, the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") and [Gold Coast-Tweed Giants](/wiki/Gold_Coast-Tweed_Giants "Gold Coast-Tweed Giants").[[9]](#cite_note-9) The Brisbane and Newcastle sides proved to be successful and popular and paved the way towards a push for a truly national competition.

This was attempted in [1995](/wiki/1995_ARL_season "1995 ARL season") with control of the premiership passing from the NSWRFL to the [Australian Rugby League](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League "Australian Rugby League") (ARL), who invited four more teams from outside NSW to participate in 1995. Ultimately this competition failed, but in its demise the National Rugby League was born, incorporating the traditional Sydney clubs, successfully compelling the Sydney market to follow the newly created national competition.

See also: [Super League war](/wiki/Super_League_war "Super League war")

The prospect of a truly national rugby league competition in addition to the introduction of pay television in Australia attracted the attention of global media organisation, [News Corporation](/wiki/News_Corporation "News Corporation"), and it followed that professional rugby league was shaken to its very foundations in the mid-1990s with the advent of the [Super League war](/wiki/Super_League_war "Super League war"). Initially a conflict over broadcasting rights, it became a dispute as to who controlled the sport and which traditional clubs would survive into the new national era, as News Limited formed their own [Super League](/wiki/Super_League_(Australia) "Super League (Australia)") and admitted some former ARL clubs, poaching players from the original ARL league with high salaries. With twenty-two teams of highly varying quality playing in two competitions that year, crowd attendances and corporate sponsorships were spread very thinly,[[10]](#cite_note-rleague1-10) and many teams found themselves in financial difficulty. The ARL undertook moves to invite the traditional clubs that had moved to the Super League competition back into a re-unified competition. Following a period of negotiation with News Corporation, on 23 September 1997 the ARL announced that it was forming a new company to conduct the competition in 1998. On 7 October News' Manaaki Ranginui announced that he was confident that there would be a single competition in 1998. On 19 December, representatives of clubs affiliated with the Australian Rugby League gathered at the [Sydney Football Stadium](/wiki/Sydney_Football_Stadium_(1988) "Sydney Football Stadium (1988)") to decide whether to accept News Limited's offer of a settlement – eventually voting in favour by 36 votes to 4.[[11]](#cite_note-11) As a result, in the following months the National Rugby League, jointly owned by the [ARL](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League "Australian Rugby League") and News Limited, was formed.

It was announced that the inaugural National Rugby League (NRL) season of [1998](/wiki/1998_NRL_season "1998 NRL season") would have 20 teams competing, 19 remaining Super League and ARL teams plus the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"), who were created by Super League for their 1998 season. Clubs on both sides of the war were shut down. Super League decided to close the [Hunter Mariners](/wiki/Hunter_Mariners "Hunter Mariners") and the financially ruined [Perth Reds](/wiki/Perth_Reds "Perth Reds"), who were $10 million in debt at the end of 1997,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] while the ARL decided to close down the [South Queensland Crushers](/wiki/South_Queensland_Crushers "South Queensland Crushers"), who were also in severe financial trouble.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Additionally, at the end of 1998 the NRL decided to close down former Super League club, the [Adelaide Rams](/wiki/Adelaide_Rams "Adelaide Rams") and former ARL club, the [Gold Coast Chargers](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Chargers "Gold Coast Chargers"), despite the Gold Coast franchise being one of the few clubs to make a profit during the Super League war.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 1998–2002: Rationalisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1998–2002: Rationalisation")]

One condition of the peace agreement between the ARL and News Limited was that there would be a 14-team competition in 2000. The 20 clubs that played in 1998 would be assessed on various items such as sponsorship, crowds, on-field success and the like. It was also announced that clubs that merged would receive a large sum of money, as well as a guaranteed position in the 2000 NRL Competition. The [St. George Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Dragons "St. George Dragons") and the [Illawarra Steelers](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers") were the first clubs to take up the offer, forming the joint-venture [St. George Illawarra Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Illawarra_Dragons "St. George Illawarra Dragons") at the end of the 1998 season.

The [1999 Grand Final](/wiki/1999_NRL_season "1999 NRL season") brought about a new official world record attendance for a game of rugby league. 107,999 spectators saw the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") defeat the newly created [St. George Illawarra Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Illawarra_Dragons "St. George Illawarra Dragons") in the decider at [Stadium Australia](/wiki/Stadium_Australia "Stadium Australia").

[Balmain](/wiki/Balmain_Tigers "Balmain Tigers") and [Western Suburbs](/wiki/Western_Suburbs_Magpies "Western Suburbs Magpies") formed the joint-venture club, the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") at the end of 1999, while [North Sydney](/wiki/North_Sydney_Bears "North Sydney Bears") and [Manly Warringah](/wiki/Manly_Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly Warringah Sea Eagles") created the ill-fated [Northern Eagles](/wiki/Northern_Eagles "Northern Eagles"). As part of another image makeover, a number of teams also released new club logos. The most notable of these was the Sydney Roosters, dropping the *City* section of their name for the 2000 season and beyond. [Souths](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs") were controversially axed from the competition at the end of 1999 for failing to meet the criteria.

This move was highly controversial and on 12 November 2000 about 80,000 marched in protest at their continued exclusion. South Sydney challenged the decision in the Federal Court claiming that the NRL agreement was exclusionary, intended to unfairly exclude South Sydney, and breached the Trade Practices Act. Justice Paul Finn ruled that the agreement did not specifically exclude any club and dismissed the Rabbitohs' claims for re-instatement into the national competition. Souths appealed this decision and were re-admitted into the competition in 2002.

The Auckland Warriors experienced much financial hardship in the early part of the decade, ultimately collapsing before being resurrected as the New Zealand Warriors for the 2001 season. They made the grand final in 2002 and then again in 2011, losing both encounters to the Sydney Roosters and the re-instated Manly Warringah Sea Eagles, respectively.

In 2001, Australia's largest telecommunications provider Telstra became naming rights sponsor of the NRL, with the competition's name becoming the NRL Telstra Premiership, while in 2002 [David Gallop](/wiki/David_Gallop "David Gallop") took over the CEO role from [David Moffett](/wiki/David_Moffett "David Moffett"), and the competition has become more and more popular each season.

In 2001 the NRL Grand Final started to be played on Sunday nights, a shift from the traditional Sunday afternoon slot used for over a decade prior.

### 2003–2010: Record popularity, expansion and centenary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2003–2010: Record popularity, expansion and centenary")]

The 2003 season was widely regarded as the most successful since the beginning of the National Rugby League in 1998. The Manly Warringah Rugby League Football Club took over the NRL licence from the [Northern Eagles](/wiki/Northern_Eagles "Northern Eagles") franchise, after the financial bankruptcy of the North Sydney faction made the joint-venture untenable. The [Penrith Panthers](/wiki/Penrith_Panthers "Penrith Panthers") rose from the bottom of the table to win the Premiership, while the Brisbane club returned to [Lang Park](/wiki/Suncorp_Stadium "Suncorp Stadium") mid-year. Season 2004 proved even more successful than 2003, with the [North Queensland Cowboys](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys") going from 11th position in 2003 to third in 2004, narrowly missing out on a maiden Grand Final berth.

Crowd average records were broken in 2003, 2004 and 2005.[[10]](#cite_note-rleague1-10) In 2005, the NRL reached record levels of popularity for its competition. Total crowds for the competition season almost reached the figures for the last year of the competition conducted by the ARL competition of 1995, prior to the Super League war. The average attendance record remained until 2010.[[10]](#cite_note-rleague1-10) From 2004 to 2005, there was a 39% increase in sponsorship, a 41% increase in merchandise royalties, and a 12% increase in playing participation.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2005, [Business Review Weekly](/wiki/BRW_(magazine) "BRW (magazine)") ranked the NRL 497 in revenue of Australian private companies, with revenue of A$66.1m (+7%) with 35 employees. In 2004, Canterbury-Bankstown put a year of turmoil and disgrace at the aftermath of the alleged rape scandal to hold aloft the NRL trophy and give the club their first premiership since 1995. In 2005, a record national audience of 4.1 million tuned in to watch the grand final between the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") and the [North Queensland Cowboys](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The [2006 Grand Final](/wiki/2006_NRL_Grand_Final "2006 NRL Grand Final") was won by the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") over the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"), 15–8. The matchup was a significant milestone in the history of the NRL, as two interstate teams (teams not from New South Wales, the "heartland" of the NRL) contested the grand final for the first time. In the city of Melbourne, whose team was playing in their second grand final, the game's television ratings were higher than in Sydney where the game was played.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Crowds were down on 2005, however were better than any other year prior to that.

In its tenth season the NRL returned to having a club based on the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland"), Queensland with the inclusion of the [Gold Coast Titans](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Titans "Gold Coast Titans"). The Titans were the first professional sporting team to occupy the Gold Coast since 1998, when the [Gold Coast Chargers](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Chargers "Gold Coast Chargers") were one of the teams removed during the NRL's rationalisation process between the end of the Super League war and the 2000 season.

The 2007 season saw the return of Monday Night Football and the inclusion of two Friday night games. Both of which turned out to be ratings successes. Another change from the previous seasons was a reduction in the number of byes per team in the season. With an odd number of teams contesting between 2002 and 2006, the draw meant that at least one team would have to have a bye each weekend. With the inclusion of the 16th team for the 2007 season, the National Rugby League had the option of reverting to back to the system used between 2000 and 2001 where every team played each round. That system was not used however, with teams were given just a single bye during the year, grouped in periods that will assist clubs around representative fixtures.

The opening round saw two matches at Brisbane's [Lang Park](/wiki/Suncorp_Stadium "Suncorp Stadium"), the first featuring reigning champions Brisbane against fellow Queensland side North Queensland, while the second match featured the new club, the Gold Coast, playing St. George Illawarra. The weather during the middle of the season was less than ideal, with cyclonic conditions severely affecting many NRL games played in Sydney and Newcastle.

The 2007 finals series saw the [South Sydney Rabbitohs](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs") return to finals football for the first time in decades. The season culminated on 30 September 2007, with the grand final contested between Manly and Melbourne. Melbourne won the title 34–8 and the grand final achieved the honour of being the most watched television show in Australia in 2007.[[15]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-15)

Throughout 2008, the NRL celebrated 100 years since rugby league was introduced into Australia, with several initiatives to recognise the important milestone, including an extensive marketing campaign called the 'Centenary of Rugby League'. The competition began in March, with a special Heritage round held in mid-April, coinciding with the first round of competition played in 1908.

At a Gala event on 17 April 2008 the [Team of the Century](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League%27s_Team_of_the_Century "Australian Rugby League's Team of the Century") was announced, being:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| * Full-back: [Clive Churchill](/wiki/Clive_Churchill "Clive Churchill") * Wingers: [Ken Irvine](/wiki/Ken_Irvine "Ken Irvine"), [Brian Bevan](/wiki/Brian_Bevan "Brian Bevan") * Centres: [Reg Gasnier](/wiki/Reg_Gasnier "Reg Gasnier"), [Mal Meninga](/wiki/Mal_Meninga "Mal Meninga") * Five-eighth: [Wally Lewis](/wiki/Wally_Lewis "Wally Lewis") * Half-back: [Andrew Johns](/wiki/Andrew_Johns "Andrew Johns") * Lock: [John Raper](/wiki/Johnny_Raper "Johnny Raper") | * Second Row: [Norm Provan](/wiki/Norm_Provan "Norm Provan"), [Ron Coote](/wiki/Ron_Coote "Ron Coote") * Props: [Arthur Beetson](/wiki/Arthur_Beetson "Arthur Beetson"), [Duncan Hall](/wiki/Duncan_Hall "Duncan Hall") * Hooker: [Noel Kelly](/wiki/Noel_Kelly_(rugby_league) "Noel Kelly (rugby league)") * Reserves: [Graeme Langlands](/wiki/Graeme_Langlands "Graeme Langlands"), [Dally Messenger](/wiki/Dally_Messenger "Dally Messenger"), [Bob Fulton](/wiki/Bob_Fulton "Bob Fulton"), [Frank Burge](/wiki/Frank_Burge "Frank Burge") * Coach: [Jack Gibson](/wiki/Jack_Gibson_(rugby_league) "Jack Gibson (rugby league)").[[16]](#cite_note-16) |

For the second year in a row, the grand final was played between the Melbourne Storm and the Manly Warringah Sea Eagles, in the NRL's first ever twilight decider. The Manly club took out the premiership game 40–0, setting the record for the highest winning margin in a grand final (although the club formerly known as St. George Dragons were beaten 38–0 in 1975 and using the modern point scale of 4-point tries, this would amount to 46–0.) Furthermore, it was the first time a team had been kept scoreless in a Grand Final since 1978.

The 2009 season marked the beginning of the second century of rugby league in Australia.
The [grand final](/wiki/2009_NRL_Grand_Final "2009 NRL Grand Final") that year was played between the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") and once again [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"). Melbourne defeated Parramatta 23 – 16 to win the premiership to make it two premierships out of the last four grand finals for the Melbourne side.

In 2010 the Inaugural All Stars Match was held on 13 February, in conjunction with the Sorry Day reconciliation anniversary to promote rugby league's long association and involvement with the Aboriginal community. The first match saw the Indigenous All Stars beat the NRL All Stars 16–12. The success of this event has seen it become a recurring fixture on the rugby league calendar with Queensland awarded the hosting rights for the next three years.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The 29th State of Origin series was also played featuring the world's first live free-to-air 3D TV broadcast.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Queensland later made further history by winning an unprecedented fifth series in a row, and winning the 2010 series by a scoreline of 3–0, their first Origin whitewash since 1995.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 2010 the NRL set a record total season average attendance of 17,367 per game and a record total season aggregate attendance of 3,490,778.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

During the 2010 finals series, the second qualifying match between the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") and [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") became the first McIntyre system final to go into extra time, with the One Hundred Minute Epic described in media circles as one of the greatest of the modern era.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The 2010 [grand final](/wiki/2010_NRL_Grand_Final "2010 NRL Grand Final") was played between the [St. George Illawarra Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Illawarra_Dragons "St. George Illawarra Dragons") and the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters"). St. George Illawarra won 32–8. This was the first premiership won by the club in its eleven-year existence and the first time in 31 years for the St. George part of the joint venture.

### 2010–2019: Establishment of the ARLC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2010–2019: Establishment of the ARLC")]

After several years of preparation and build up, on 14 December 2010 the Australian Rugby League and News Corporation agreed upon a constitutional framework paving the way for the establishment of a new and independent commission to govern the sport in Australia. The negotiations of such a framework became drawn out over establishing details, primarily of sponsorship, media rights, funding of state bodies, funding of the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"), debate over News Ltd private ownership of clubs, and also of individual appointments to the new body.
The 2011 grand final was contested between the [Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles](/wiki/Manly-Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles") and the [New Zealand Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors"). This was the second grand final involving the New Zealand Warriors. Manly recorded a 24–10 win claiming their second premiership under head coach [Des Hasler](/wiki/Des_Hasler "Des Hasler").

On 10 February 2012, the independent commission, known as the [Australian Rugby League Commission](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League_Commission "Australian Rugby League Commission") assumed control of all levels of the game, replacing former state based boards and assuming full control of the NRL from the NRL partnership (comprising the previous ARL board and [News Limited](/wiki/News_Corp_Australia "News Corp Australia")).[[23]](#cite_note-23)
The current Chairman is Peter V'landys AM. The 2012 Grand Final involved the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs") and the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"), this was the first time since 2008 that the first and second placed teams contested the grand final. The [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") recorded a 14–4 victory thus achieving their second premiership and claiming some vengeance for the stripped premierships.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Dragons_vs_Roosters%2C_Anzac_Day_2018.jpg/250px-Dragons_vs_Roosters%2C_Anzac_Day_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dragons_vs_Roosters,_Anzac_Day_2018.jpg)

Pre-match formalities taking place prior to the Dragons vs Roosters Anzac Day clash in 2018

The 2013 season saw the resurgence of foundation clubs the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") and the [South Sydney Rabbitohs](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs") with both clubs finishing first and second place respectively. The NRL was also left with a black eye after the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks") were caught using performance-enhancing substances by ASADA dating back to 2011. The 2013 Grand Final was played between the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") and the [Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles](/wiki/Manly-Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles") with the Roosters recording a 26–18 victory.

The 2014 season started with the introduction of the Auckland Nines and was marred by the handing down of a million dollar fine to the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks") for their role in the ASADA scandal while head coach Shane Flanagan was suspended for the entirety of the 2014 season. The 2014 Grand Final was contested by the [South Sydney Rabbitohs](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs") and the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs"). This was South Sydney's first grand final appearance since 1971. South Sydney would break their 43-year premiership drought with a 30–6 victory.

The 2015 season saw the return of the Queensland juggernauts, The [North Queensland Cowboys](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys") and the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") who would finish inside the top four and contest the 2015 Grand Final. The 2015 Grand Final is considered by many to be the greatest grand final of all time as a high intensity match ended in dramatic circumstances when North Queensland scored as the full-time siren sounded. Johnathan Thurston would miss the conversion from the sideline as the ball hit the post thus sending the match into golden point, the first golden point grand final. Ben Hunt would drop the ball on the kick-off and hand the North Queensland side the chance to win which was converted as Johnathan Thurston successfully kicked a field goal to win the match 17–16.

The 2016 season saw the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") return to the minor premiership position while the [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders") returned to the top four for the first time since the 2003. The [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks") and reigning premiers North Queensland would fill out the top four. The season continued a trend of the minor premiership not being decided to the final round, the fourth consecutive year. The 2016 Grand Final was contested between the Melbourne Storm and the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks"), it was the Cronulla's fourth grand final appearance while it was the seventh for the Melbourne club. The match was a tight affair and was not settled until after the siren had sounded. The Cronulla-Sutherland claimed their first premiership ending a famous 49-year drought by a score of 14–12.

Following the success generated by the [2017 Women's Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/2017_Women%27s_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2017 Women's Rugby League World Cup"), a [national women's league](/wiki/NRL_Women%27s_Premiership "NRL Women's Premiership") was established, and the [first season](/wiki/2018_NRL_Women%27s_season "2018 NRL Women's season") commenced in September 2018 comprising four clubs aligned to existing NRL clubs.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### 2020–2021: COVID affected seasons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2020–2021: COVID affected seasons")]

On 22 March 2020, during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the NRL became one of the last major sports in the world to continue playing games. Following the imposition of the first COVID-19 restrictions, the NRL initially announced it would be moving forward with the season.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The decision prompted a wave of condemnation online, including from Australian personalities and sport people.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The following day after Queensland closed its borders, the NRL announced that the season would have to be suspended indefinitely.[[27]](#cite_note-27) It was feared that some teams would not survive this period without income, as it was foreseen as a financial catastrophe.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

On 9 April 2020, ARL Commissioner [Wayne Pearce](/wiki/Wayne_Pearce "Wayne Pearce") announced the NRL would return on 28 May.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The announcement came under heavy criticism from the [Government of New South Wales](/wiki/Government_of_New_South_Wales "Government of New South Wales") and health officials, with Premier [Gladys Berejiklian](/wiki/Gladys_Berejiklian "Gladys Berejiklian") saying she had not approved a return.[[31]](#cite_note-guard-green-light-31) However, the NRL released documents signed by NSW Police Commissioner Mick Fuller, showing approval for the NRL to continue without crowds.[[31]](#cite_note-guard-green-light-31) The competition ended up kicking off on that date, with the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") defeating the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") 34–6 at [Lang Park](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park") behind closed doors.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The match drew the highest TV ratings for a regular season game since 2014.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The NRL returned a full two weeks before any other sport in Australia, and were the first sport to return to play worldwide that actually ceased play.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In October 2021, after months of speculation, the NRL and the ARLC announced that the competition would expand to 17 teams, with the admission of the [Dolphins](/wiki/Dolphins_(NRL) "Dolphins (NRL)"), based out of the suburb of Redcliffe in greater Brisbane, for the 2023 season.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### 2022 – present: Further expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2022 – present: Further expansion")]

Following the introduction of the Dolphins in 2023, NRL executives spoke openly about the desire to further expand the competition. On 12 March 2023, reports emerged that the ARL Commission was working towards a 20-team competition, and sought to achieve this target before the [2032 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2032_Summer_Olympics "2032 Summer Olympics") in Brisbane.[[36]](#cite_note-36) ARLC CEO Andrew Abdo and chairman Peter V’landys confirmed the plan would be to add three teams to the NRL by 2030, of which up to two could be based outside of Australia.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In October 2024 a [Perth](/wiki/Perth,_Western_Australia "Perth, Western Australia"), [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia") bid, in consultation with the [North Sydney Bears](/wiki/North_Sydney_Bears "North Sydney Bears") and backed by the Western Australian Government, was reported however this was rejected 2 weeks later by the NRL commission over fears the consortium would be unable to fund the $20 million expansion fee.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) By November, talks had resumed and the Western Bears were largely expected to be announced as the NRL's 18th team, entering in the 2027 season.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

On 12 December 2024, ARLC chairman Peter V'Landy's, Prime Minister [Anthony Albanese](/wiki/Anthony_Albanese "Anthony Albanese") and Prime Minister [James Marape](/wiki/James_Marape "James Marape") announced a team based in [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea") would enter the NRL in the 2028 season. The $600 million venture is funded by the Australian Government for a ten-year period and was made in conjunction with a parallel agreement concerning "strategic trust" and security between the two countries. The team will be based in the capital city of [Port Moresby](/wiki/Port_Moresby "Port Moresby") and play home matches at the [National Football Stadium](/wiki/PNG_Football_Stadium "PNG Football Stadium").[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-nrl.com-42) The bid would be partially funded by the Australian Government, providing $600M over 10 years to develop the competition in PNG[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Having selected a [Papua New Guinea NRL team](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea_NRL_team "Papua New Guinea NRL team") as its eighteenth team, in April 2025, the NRL originally put negotiations on hold after deciding to postpone the introduction of a 19th team.[[44]](#cite_note-44) However, by 24 April 2025, the NRL agreed to a $50 million deal with the Western Australian Government, thereby securing the 2027 return of the Bears as the [Perth Bears](/wiki/Perth_Bears "Perth Bears").[[45]](#cite_note-nrl-agrees-50m-deal-45)

Under the terms of the final agreement with the ARLC, the WA government has committed to spending a total of $85 million, including $35 million on grassroots rugby over the next seven years, with the remaining $50 million being $25 million on content over the next five years, $5 million to help with the costs of the new team, and $20 million will be committed to a Centre of Excellence.
The WA government will not pay a licence fee, with a promise being made that there will be a likely upgrade of their home ground, the [Perth Rectangular Stadium](/wiki/Perth_Rectangular_Stadium "Perth Rectangular Stadium").[[45]](#cite_note-nrl-agrees-50m-deal-45)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Governance")]

Since the NRL commenced in 1998 there have been six [CEOs](/wiki/CEO "CEO"), they are:

* 1998–1999: [Neil Whittaker](/wiki/Neil_Whittaker "Neil Whittaker")
* 1999–2001: [David Moffett](/wiki/David_Moffett "David Moffett")[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)
* 2002–2012: [David Gallop](/wiki/David_Gallop "David Gallop")[[48]](#cite_note-48)
* 2012–2015: [David Smith](/wiki/David_Smith_(executive) "David Smith (executive)")[[49]](#cite_note-49)
* 2016–2020: [Todd Greenberg](/wiki/Todd_Greenberg "Todd Greenberg")[[50]](#cite_note-salary-cap-1-50)
* 2020–present: Andrew Abdo[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Since the [Australian Rugby League Commission](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League_Commission "Australian Rugby League Commission") was inaugurated in 2012, there have been three chairmen:

* 2012–2017: [John Grant](/wiki/John_Grant_(rugby_league) "John Grant (rugby league)")
* 2017–2019: [Peter Beattie](/wiki/Peter_Beattie "Peter Beattie")
* 2019–present: [Peter V'landys](/wiki/Peter_V%27landys "Peter V'landys")

## Teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Teams")]

See also: [List of teams in the NSWRL/ARL/SL/NRL](/wiki/List_of_teams_in_the_NSWRL/ARL/SL/NRL "List of teams in the NSWRL/ARL/SL/NRL")

The NRL currently consists of seventeen clubs. Nine clubs are based within the Greater Sydney area (including one that also represents the [Illawarra](/wiki/Illawarra "Illawarra") region south of Sydney), another in regional [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), four in [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), and one each in [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)"), the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory"), and New Zealand. The league operates on a single group system, with no divisions or conferences and no [relegation or promotion](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation").

A total of twenty-five clubs have played in the NRL since its first season in 1998. Eleven clubs have been members for every season.

Two of the clubs currently in the NRL were founded in 1908, the first year of rugby league competition in Australia: the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") (founded as 'Eastern Suburbs') and the [South Sydney Rabbitohs](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs"). The [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") are a merger of two other foundation clubs: The [Western Suburbs Magpies](/wiki/Western_Suburbs_Magpies "Western Suburbs Magpies") and the [Balmain Tigers](/wiki/Balmain_Tigers "Balmain Tigers").

## Season structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Season structure")]

### Pre-season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Pre-season")]

The NRL pre-season typically begins in February and ends in early March. Clubs generally use this time to organise trial matches to test playing combinations. However, some clubs play for trophies: [Easts](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") and [Wests](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") play for the [Foundation Cup](/wiki/Foundation_Cup "Foundation Cup") while [South Sydney](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs") and [St. George Illawarra](/wiki/St._George_Illawarra_Dragons "St. George Illawarra Dragons") play for the [Charity Shield](/wiki/Rugby_League_Charity_Shield_(Australia) "Rugby League Charity Shield (Australia)"). The trials are typically played in cities and towns that normally don't host NRL matches. In the past a [World Sevens rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Sevens "Rugby League World Sevens") football tournament has been hosted, however this was scrapped due to injury concerns. 2023 saw the introduction of the [NRL Pre-season Challenge](/w/index.php?title=NRL_Pre-season_Challenge&action=edit&redlink=1 "NRL Pre-season Challenge (page does not exist)") to create a more structured pre-season for each club.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

#### All Stars match

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: All Stars match")]

Main article: [All Stars Match](/wiki/All_Stars_Match "All Stars Match")

Beginning in 2010, the All Stars match has become a major fixture in the NRL preseason. The teams are chosen via public voting and the two sides play for the [Arthur Beetson](/wiki/Arthur_Beetson "Arthur Beetson") Trophy. In 2018, the game was temporarily removed from the calendar due to the World Cup being played the previous year citing an excessive player workload. The match returned in 2019 and was hosted in Melbourne with the Australian Indigenous All Stars taking on the New Zealand Maori All Stars.

#### NRL Nines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: NRL Nines")]

Main article: [NRL Nines](/wiki/NRL_Nines "NRL Nines")

In 2014, the inaugural [rugby league nines](/wiki/Rugby_league_nines "Rugby league nines") tournament took place, featuring all sixteen NRL clubs. It was initially staged at Eden Park, [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"), New Zealand.[[54]](#cite_note-56) Women's nines games were also fixtured alongside the main tournament between the Australian and New Zealand national women's teams, however in later tournaments included NRLW teams. It was envisaged that the tournament would become a regular fixture each year, however, by 2018 the tournament was removed from the calendar citing an excessive player workload, it was brought back in 2020 but hasn't been played since for the same reason.

#### World Club Challenge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: World Club Challenge")]

Main article: [World Club Challenge](/wiki/World_Club_Challenge "World Club Challenge")

The World Club Challenge is an annual pre-season match played between the champion of the National Rugby League and the champion of the [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League"). The first match of its kind was the [1976 World Club Challenge](/wiki/1976_World_Club_Challenge "1976 World Club Challenge") and it was then played sporadically throughout the late 80s and 90s, including a one off Super League tournament in 1997, before becoming a regular annual pre-season fixture since 2000.

In 2015, the competition was expanded to include two exhibition games before the Challenge game. This expanded series, called the *[World Club Series](/wiki/World_Club_Series "World Club Series")*, ran from 2015 to 2017. The first two Series saw the leagues' champions joined by two invited teams from each league, resulting in a three-game series. Each invited team represented their league in a single game, played in the days leading up to the usual Challenge match. In 2017, only one invited team from each league participated, with the NRL citing tight schedules, distant travel and long seasons as an impediment to the Series. In 2018 the series was cancelled, with only the usual Challenge match played and has continued as a single match since. In 2021 and 2022 the Challenge match was cancelled because of the COVID-19 pandemic. In 2025 the event was cancelled again, with Penrith citing schedule conflicts.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

### Premiership rounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Premiership rounds")]

Main article: [NRL regular season](/wiki/NRL_regular_season "NRL regular season")

As rugby league is a winter sport in Australia, the NRL premiership season begins in early March, with games played every weekend for 27 weeks, until the start of September. In most rounds, one match is played on Thursday night, two on Friday night, three on Saturday and two on Sunday. Teams receive two competition points for a win, and one point for a draw. The bye also receives two points; a loss, no points. Teams on the ladder are ranked by competition points in descending order, then match points differential (for and against) in descending order and points percentage are used to separate teams with equal competition points and equal points differential. At the end of the regular season, the club which is ranked highest on the ladder is declared [minor premiers](/wiki/Minor_premiership "Minor premiership").

#### Rugby League Las Vegas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Rugby League Las Vegas")]

Main article: [Rugby League Las Vegas](/wiki/Rugby_League_Las_Vegas "Rugby League Las Vegas")

Beginning in the 2024 season [Rugby League Las Vegas](/wiki/Rugby_League_Las_Vegas "Rugby League Las Vegas") is an annual event held at the [Allegiant Stadium](/wiki/Allegiant_Stadium "Allegiant Stadium") in [Las Vegas](/wiki/Paradise,_Nevada "Paradise, Nevada") showcasing the sport of [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"). The event was created by the NRL to expand the audience of the competition and develop [rugby league in the United States](/wiki/Rugby_league_in_the_United_States "Rugby league in the United States"). It features two Round 1 NRL matches and from 2025, a Round 1 [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League") game as well. The event is currently contracted until 2028.[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59)

#### Magic Round

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Magic Round")]

Main article: [Magic Round (NRL)](/wiki/Magic_Round_(NRL) "Magic Round (NRL)")

In 2019, the NRL introduced [Magic Round](/wiki/Magic_Round_(NRL) "Magic Round (NRL)"), which featured all matches playing at [Lang Park](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park") in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") over the weekend. It was deemed a success and has now been a scheduled annual event since.

#### Themed rounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Themed rounds")]

Special themed weeks within the premiership rounds include [ANZAC](/wiki/ANZAC "ANZAC") Round, Heritage Round, Women in League Round, Retro Round, Beanies for Brain Cancer round, and Rivalry Round. Separate trophies between rival teams are also presented throughout the season.

In 2022, the NRL held various themed rounds, including a Pride Round (to celebrate and respect inclusivity of [LGBTQI](/wiki/LGBTQI "LGBTQI") players); Multicultural Round; ANZAC Round; Brain Cancer Round; and Women in League Round. However, following the boycott of the game by seven Manly players who refused to wear the specially designed jersey and ensuing poor publicity, it was decided not to have a Pride Round in 2023, but to stick with the other four rounds.[[58]](#cite_note-60)[[59]](#cite_note-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62)

### Mid-season representative rounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Mid-season representative rounds")]

As well as playing for their club in the premiership, NRL players are regularly selected to play in a number of representative competitions that are conducted throughout each season. The representative rounds generally occurs in a period of that runs from about the middle of April until the middle of July each year.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] These matches have included:

* The annual [Anzac Test](/wiki/Anzac_Test "Anzac Test") between [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_league_team "New Zealand national rugby league team") (April–May; 1997–2017)
* Test matches between Pacific Island Nations, played on the same weekend as the Anzac Test. These games feature matches placed between the [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands_national_rugby_league_team "Cook Islands national rugby league team"), [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji_national_rugby_league_team "Fiji national rugby league team"), [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea_national_rugby_league_team "Papua New Guinea national rugby league team"), [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa_national_rugby_league_team "Samoa national rugby league team"), and [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga_national_rugby_league_team "Tonga national rugby league team"), and includes the [Melanesian Cup](/wiki/Melanesian_Cup "Melanesian Cup") and [Polynesian Cup](/wiki/Polynesian_Cup "Polynesian Cup")
* The three match [State of Origin series](/wiki/State_of_Origin_series "State of Origin series") (June – July)
* Additionally, players from outside Oceania may travel home to take part in training camps or matches for their national side.

### Finals series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Finals series")]

Main article: [NRL finals system](/wiki/NRL_finals_system "NRL finals system")

The eight highest placed teams at the end of the regular season compete in the finals series. The system consists of a number of games between the top eight teams over four weeks in September, until only two teams remain. These two teams then contest the grand final, which is usually played on the first Sunday of October. From 1998 to 2011, the NRL used the [McIntyre final eight system](/wiki/McIntyre_final_eight_system "McIntyre final eight system") but with the introduction of the ARLC, it was decided to change to the [current format](/wiki/AFL_final_eight_system "AFL final eight system") which was perceived as fairer for teams finishing in the top four.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

### Grand final

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Grand final")]

Main article: [NRL Grand Final](/wiki/NRL_Grand_Final "NRL Grand Final")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Darren_Lockyer_and_Wayne_Bennett_%284_October_2006%2C_Brisbane%29.jpg/250px-Darren_Lockyer_and_Wayne_Bennett_%284_October_2006%2C_Brisbane%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Darren_Lockyer_and_Wayne_Bennett_(4_October_2006,_Brisbane).jpg)

[Darren Lockyer](/wiki/Darren_Lockyer "Darren Lockyer") and [Wayne Bennett](/wiki/Wayne_Bennett_(rugby_league) "Wayne Bennett (rugby league)") parade the premiership after the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos")' [Grand Final victory in 2006](/wiki/2006_NRL_Grand_Final "2006 NRL Grand Final").

The [NRL Grand Final](/wiki/NRL_Grand_Final "NRL Grand Final"), which determines the season's premiers, is one of Australia's major sporting events and is one of the largest attended club championship events in the world. It has been contested at Sydney's [Stadium Australia](/wiki/Stadium_Australia "Stadium Australia") each year since 1999, with the exception of 2021 when it was played at Brisbane's [Lang Park](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park") due to the COVID pandemic.[[62]](#cite_note-64) The first year it was held at Stadium Australia, the NRL Grand Final broke the record for attendance at an Australian rugby league game, with 107,999 people attending.[[63]](#cite_note-65)

The game itself is usually preceded by an opening ceremony featuring entertainment and the singing of the national anthem by well-known Australasian and international musical acts. At the conclusion of the grand final there is a presentation ceremony where the winning team are awarded the Provan-Summons Trophy and [premiership rings](/wiki/Championship_ring "Championship ring") to each player and the head coach.[[64]](#cite_note-66) The player judged to be the man-of-the-match by the [Australian national team](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") selectors is awarded the prestigious [Clive Churchill Medal](/wiki/Clive_Churchill_Medal "Clive Churchill Medal") and the [Prime Minister of Australia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Australia "Prime Minister of Australia") is typically on-hand to hand the trophy to the winning captain.

### Post-season internationals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Post-season internationals")]

Following the premiership's conclusion other representative matches occasionally occur. These have included:

* The quadrennial [World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup")
* The [Four Nations](/wiki/Rugby_League_Four_Nations "Rugby League Four Nations") tournament – Australia, New Zealand and England plus another qualifier
* The [Prime Minister's XIII match](/wiki/Prime_Minister%27s_XIII "Prime Minister's XIII") in Papua New Guinea
* The [Rugby League World Cup 9s](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup_9s "Rugby League World Cup 9s")
* Additional test matches between Australia and New Zealand.
* The [Rugby League Pacific Championships](/wiki/Rugby_League_Pacific_Championships "Rugby League Pacific Championships")
* [Tours](/wiki/List_of_rugby_league_tours "List of rugby league tours") to [European Rugby League](/wiki/European_Rugby_League "European Rugby League") countries, or hosting tours of European Rugby League national teams

## Players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Players")]

National Rugby League footballers are among Australasia's most famous athletes[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], commanding multimillion-dollar playing contracts and sponsorship deals. Each club in the NRL has a "top squad" of twenty-five players, who are signed under a salary cap. For the most part, the players who play in NRL matches are sourced from these top squads. During a season the need may arise for a club to use players outside these 25, in which case players are usually sourced from the club's corresponding [NRL Under-20s](/wiki/NRL_Under-20s "NRL Under-20s") team or a [feeder club](/wiki/Farm_team "Farm team") in the [New South Wales Cup](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Cup "New South Wales Cup") or [Queensland Cup](/wiki/Queensland_Cup "Queensland Cup").[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-68)[[67]](#cite_note-69)[[68]](#cite_note-70)

### Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Demographics")]

In the recent years, the influence of [Polynesian](/wiki/Polynesians "Polynesians") players on the NRL has grown, with figures from the 2011 season showing that 35% of NRL players and over 45% of NRL Under-20s players are of Polynesian background.[[69]](#cite_note-71) This increase in Polynesian players has been blamed for the decline of Indigenous players, dropping from 21% in the 1990s to 11% for the 2009 season.[[70]](#cite_note-72)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Ben_Barba.jpg/250px-Ben_Barba.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ben_Barba.jpg)

[Ben Barba](/wiki/Ben_Barba "Ben Barba") at the Dally M awards

The first [Indigenous Australian](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") to play in the NSWRL/NRL was [New South Wales Rugby League premiership](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League_premiership "New South Wales Rugby League premiership") player [George Green](/wiki/George_Green_(rugby_league) "George Green (rugby league)"), who debuted in 1909. Since that time, many high-profile indigenous athletes have played in the competition, including [Arthur Beetson](/wiki/Arthur_Beetson "Arthur Beetson") (the first Aboriginal person to captain an Australian national team in any sport[[71]](#cite_note-73)) and former [Test match](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") representatives [Johnathan Thurston](/wiki/Johnathan_Thurston "Johnathan Thurston") and [Greg Inglis](/wiki/Greg_Inglis "Greg Inglis").

At the elite level of the game, [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") represented 35% of the roster for the Kangaroos, 21% of players at the 2012 State of Origin series, 12% of NRL players and a further 8% of the NRL Under-20s players.[[72]](#cite_note-74) By way of comparison, 2.3% of the Australian population identified themselves as Indigenous in the 2006 Australian census.[[73]](#cite_note-75)

To celebrate the strong Indigenous ties to the game, the NRL holds a preseason [All Stars](/wiki/All_Stars_Match "All Stars Match") game, featuring a team made up of Indigenous Australians playing a publicly voted team consisting of the non-Indigenous players, and an Indigenous Leadership Group has formed, consisting of the game's elite Indigenous players. The Leadership Group hosts regular multi-day camps where all Indigenous NRL players are invited to learn more about Indigenous culture.[[74]](#cite_note-76)

Both the All Stars match and the Leadership Group were ideas pioneered by former player [Preston Campbell](/wiki/Preston_Campbell "Preston Campbell"),[[75]](#cite_note-77) with the All Stars [man of the match](/wiki/Man_of_the_match "Man of the match") receiving the Preston Campbell medal.

### Salary cap

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Salary cap")]

Main article: [National Rugby League salary cap](/wiki/National_Rugby_League_salary_cap "National Rugby League salary cap")

A salary cap was introduced to the [NSWRL](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League "New South Wales Rugby League") in 1990 to even the playing field of teams in the [Winfield Cup](/wiki/Winfield_Cup "Winfield Cup").[[76]](#cite_note-WIP-78)
As of 2013 the club grant is $7.1 million which covers the salary cap of $5.85 million and a minimum wage for $80,000 for the top twenty-five players at each club.[[77]](#cite_note-79) The salary cap increased to $6.3 million in 2014, $6.55 million in 2015, $6.8 million by 2016 and $7 million in 2017.[[78]](#cite_note-80)

The cap is actively policed[[79]](#cite_note-81) and penalties for clubs found to have breached the NRL salary cap regulations include fines of lesser of half the amount involved or $500,000 and/or deduction of premiership points. For example, six clubs were fined for minor infractions in 2003. These infractions are usually technical in nature, and can sometimes be affected by third-party factors such as loss of sponsorship revenue affecting an allowance. During the 2007 season the NRL implemented ways of creating a fair and more beneficial cap for players and clubs.

In 2010, following the Melbourne Storm salary cap scandal, the NRL introduced requirements for players and their agents to sign statutory declarations pledging their contracts comply with salary cap regulations, where previously only club chairmen and chief executives did so for biannual salary cap audits.[[80]](#cite_note-82)

**Major breaches of the cap**

See also: [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs salary cap breach](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs_salary_cap_breach "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs salary cap breach"), [Melbourne Storm salary cap breach](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm_salary_cap_breach "Melbourne Storm salary cap breach"), and [Parramatta Eels salary cap breach](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels_salary_cap_breach "Parramatta Eels salary cap breach")

In 2002, the [Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs") were fined the maximum of $500,000 and deducted all 37 premiership points received during the season after it was found that they had committed serious and systematic breaches of the salary cap totalling $2.13 million over the past three years, including $750,000 in 2001 and $920,000 in 2002; these were described by NRL chief executive [David Gallop](/wiki/David_Gallop "David Gallop") as "exceptional in both its size and its deliberate and ongoing nature". The points penalty meant that the club won the 2002 [wooden spoon](/wiki/Wooden_spoon_(award) "Wooden spoon (award)") ([Souths](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs") would have finished last if not for the breaches), and as the club had been leading the competition table prior to the imposition of the penalties, this was a shattering outcome for the club and its fans. Two senior club officials were jailed for fraud as a result of these breaches.

In 2005, the [New Zealand Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors") were fined $430,000 and were ordered to start the 2006 season with a four premiership point deficit and cut their payroll by $450,000 after club officials revealed that their former management had exceeded the salary cap by $1.1 million over the last two years. The points penalty meant that the Warriors missed a finals berth in 2006.

On 22 April 2010, following revelations by an internal [whistleblower](/wiki/Whistleblower "Whistleblower") and investigation by the NRL, Storm officials revealed that the club had committed serious and systematic breaches of the salary cap regulations between 2006 and 2010 by running a well-organized dual contract and bookkeeping system that concealed a total of $3.78 million in payments made to players outside of the salary cap from the NRL. This included: $303,000 in 2006, $459,000 in 2007, $957,000 in 2008, $1.021 million in 2009 and $1.04 million in 2010. As a result, the club was stripped of the 2007 and 2009 premierships, 2006–2008 minor premierships and the following year, the 2010 World Club Challenge trophy. They were also fined a then Australian sporting record of $1.689 million ($1.1 million in NRL prize money which was equally distributed between the remaining 15 clubs, $89,000 in prize money from the World Club Challenge which was distributed to the [Leeds Rhinos](/wiki/Leeds_Rhinos "Leeds Rhinos"), and a maximum of $500,000 for breaching the salary cap regulations). In addition they were ordered to cut their payroll by $1.0125 million, deducted all eight premiership points received during the 2010 season and barred from receiving premiership points for the remainder of the 2010 season. The points penalty meant that the club won the 2010 wooden spoon. The former directors attempted unsuccessful legal action against the penalties which collapsed, and the club also had to pay the NRLs legal costs. The matter was also referred to ASIC, the Australian Tax Office, the Victorian State Revenue Office, and the Victoria Police to investigate possible fraud however by May 2011 these investigations were closed.[[81]](#cite_note-83) The players themselves were found to have not done anything wrong during this time so individual awards are recognised in addition, the players were still eligible for Test/State of Origin selection.

Prior to the start of the 2016 NRL season, the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") faced the prospect with starting the season on −4 points due to salary cap indiscretions in 2015, however the NRL was satisfied with governance changes at the Eels and no points were deducted.[[82]](#cite_note-84) However, it was revealed in March that third-party payments had been made by several companies to several players, which is strictly prohibited in the NRL.[[83]](#cite_note-85) On 3 May 2016, NRL CEO Todd Greenberg announced that the club would be docked the twelve competition points they have accrued so far this season, as well as fined $1 million and stripped of the [2016 NRL Auckland Nines](/wiki/2016_NRL_Auckland_Nines "2016 NRL Auckland Nines") title it won in February.[[50]](#cite_note-salary-cap-1-50) In addition, the NRL also announced that the Eels would not be able to accrue any further competition points until they fall under the salary cap, which they were reported to be $500,000 over as of 3 May 2016.[[84]](#cite_note-86) Five officials, including chairman Steve Sharp, deputy chairman Tom Issa, director Peter Serrao, chief executive John Boulous and football manager Daniel Anderson, were also sacked.[[85]](#cite_note-87) On 9 July, after over 2 months of club officials contesting the preliminary penalties, Parramatta were handed their punishment with the addition of their for/against points tally accumulated from rounds 1–9 being deducted.[[86]](#cite_note-88)

In 2018, the Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles were fined $750,000, two officials (Neil Bare and Joe Kelly were suspended and a $660,000 penalty was applied to the salary cap for 2018 and the next year for breaches of the salary cap). Manly was proved by the NRL chief executive that third-party payments and deals were made to attract more players to the club.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

**Judiciary and Integrity Unit**

Main article: [NRL Judiciary](/wiki/NRL_Judiciary "NRL Judiciary")

The [NRL Judiciary](/wiki/NRL_Judiciary "NRL Judiciary") is made up of former players who convene in three-man panels to rule on on-field incidents. The judiciary is currently chaired by [Wollongong](/wiki/Wollongong "Wollongong") district court judge Paul Conlon and made up of former players [Mal Cochrane](/wiki/Mal_Cochrane "Mal Cochrane"), [Michael Buettner](/wiki/Michael_Buettner "Michael Buettner"), [Bradley Clyde](/wiki/Bradley_Clyde "Bradley Clyde"), [Sean Garlick](/wiki/Sean_Garlick "Sean Garlick"), [Don McKinnon](/wiki/Don_McKinnon_(rugby_league) "Don McKinnon (rugby league)") and [Bob Lindner](/wiki/Bob_Lindner "Bob Lindner").[[87]](#cite_note-89)[[88]](#cite_note-90)

An integrity Unit was formed on 7 February 2013 and was headed by former Federal Court judge Tony Whitlam, since 2022 the new head of the NRL integrity Unit is former Manly Sea-Eagles front-row forward, Jason King.[[89]](#cite_note-91)

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Awards")]

### Provan-Summons trophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Provan-Summons trophy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Provan-Summons_Trophy.jpg/250px-Provan-Summons_Trophy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Provan-Summons_Trophy.jpg)

The 2017 edition of the Provan-Summons Trophy which was awarded to the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm").

The Provan-Summons trophy is the NRL's main prize, awarded to the team that wins the premiership. Its sculptured design is similar to the [Winfield Cup](/wiki/Winfield_Cup "Winfield Cup") trophy, which was introduced for the [1982 NSWRFL season](/wiki/1982_NSWRFL_season "1982 NSWRFL season"). It is a three-dimensional cast of a famous photo called *[The Gladiators](/wiki/The_Gladiators_(photograph) "The Gladiators (photograph)")*,[[90]](#cite_note-92) which depicts a mud-soaked [Norm Provan](/wiki/Norm_Provan "Norm Provan") of [St. George](/wiki/St._George_Dragons "St. George Dragons") and [Arthur Summons](/wiki/Arthur_Summons "Arthur Summons") of [Western Suburbs](/wiki/Western_Suburbs_Magpies "Western Suburbs Magpies") embracing after the [1963 NSWRFL season](/wiki/1963_NSWRFL_season "1963 NSWRFL season")'s Grand Final. It was not officially named the Provan-Summons Trophy until 2013, the 50th anniversary of the 1963 Grand Final.[[91]](#cite_note-93) The trophy is awarded following each grand final to the captain of the winning club. The trophy was mysteriously broken during grand final celebrations in 2021.[[92]](#cite_note-94)

Each player from the premiership winning side are also awarded [Premiership Rings](/wiki/Premiership_ring "Premiership ring").

### J. J. Giltinan Shield

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: J. J. Giltinan Shield")]

Created in 1951, the [J. J. Giltinan Shield](/wiki/J._J._Giltinan_Shield "J. J. Giltinan Shield") is awarded to the club that finished first on the NRL ladder after the premiership season concludes. Typically this team is known as the [minor premiers](/wiki/Minor_premiers "Minor premiers").

### Clive Churchill medal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Clive Churchill medal")]

The [Clive Churchill Medal](/wiki/Clive_Churchill_Medal "Clive Churchill Medal") is awarded to the most outstanding player in a grand final. It is named after former [Australian](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") fullback [Clive Churchill](/wiki/Clive_Churchill "Clive Churchill").

### Dally M awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Dally M awards")]

The [Dally M Medal](/wiki/Dally_M_Medal "Dally M Medal") is the highlight of the [Dally M Awards](/wiki/Dally_M_Awards "Dally M Awards") and was named after [Henry Herbert 'Dally' Messenger](/wiki/Dally_Messenger "Dally Messenger"), who was instrumental in the establishment of rugby league football in Australia.
The awards were introduced in 1980 by News Limited.
The Dally M has been the official player-of-the-year award, and the highest individual honour in Australian rugby league, since the unification of the game in 1998. Before 1998 the highest award was the [Rothmans Medal](/wiki/Rothmans_Medal "Rothmans Medal").

The medal is awarded, usually by the Australian Prime Minister, at the annual Dally M Awards night.
As well as honouring the best and fairest player of the year, the Dally M awards recognise the premier player in each position, the best coach and the most outstanding rookie of the season.

### The Immortals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: The Immortals")]

Originally [The Immortals](/wiki/The_Immortals_(rugby_league) "The Immortals (rugby league)") were four players named by the sport's major Australian magazine *[Rugby League Week](/wiki/Rugby_League_Week "Rugby League Week")* as the nation's greatest ever.

Established in 1981, the group was four former [Test](/wiki/Test_match_(rugby_league) "Test match (rugby league)") captains, [Clive Churchill](/wiki/Clive_Churchill "Clive Churchill"), [Bob Fulton](/wiki/Bob_Fulton "Bob Fulton"), [Reg Gasnier](/wiki/Reg_Gasnier "Reg Gasnier"), [Johnny Raper](/wiki/Johnny_Raper "Johnny Raper") and were those appointed by *[Rugby League Week](/wiki/Rugby_League_Week "Rugby League Week")* between 1981 and 2012. [Wally Lewis](/wiki/Wally_Lewis "Wally Lewis"), [Graeme Langlands](/wiki/Graeme_Langlands "Graeme Langlands") added in 1999 [Arthur Beetson](/wiki/Arthur_Beetson "Arthur Beetson") 2003 [Andrew Johns](/wiki/Andrew_Johns "Andrew Johns") 2012

Following the demise of *[Rugby League Week](/wiki/Rugby_League_Week "Rugby League Week")*, the [Australian Rugby League Commission](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League_Commission "Australian Rugby League Commission") took ownership of [The Immortals](/wiki/The_Immortals_(rugby_league) "The Immortals (rugby league)") concept and expanded the group on 1 August 2018 by inclusion of [Mal Meninga](/wiki/Mal_Meninga "Mal Meninga"), [Norm Provan](/wiki/Norm_Provan "Norm Provan"), [Frank Burge](/wiki/Frank_Burge "Frank Burge"), [Dave Brown](/wiki/Dave_Brown_(rugby_league,_born_1913) "Dave Brown (rugby league, born 1913)") and [Dally Messenger](/wiki/Dally_Messenger "Dally Messenger").[[93]](#cite_note-95) [Ron Coote](/wiki/Ron_Coote "Ron Coote") was named the 14th immortal in 2024.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Queensland Rugby League's Team of the Century")]

#### ANZ and Pacific

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: ANZ and Pacific")]

Within Australia all matches are broadcast on both the [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") and [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports_(Australia) "Fox Sports (Australia)") through 2027. The Fox Sports contract was signed in May 2020 and the NRL extended their contract on Nine in December 2021 to match the length of the contract with Fox Sports through 2027.[[95]](#cite_note-97)[[96]](#cite_note-98) In New Zealand all matches, including representative fixtures such as the [All Stars Match](/wiki/All_Stars_Match "All Stars Match") and the [State of Origin series](/wiki/State_of_Origin_series "State of Origin series"), are shown live on [Sky Sport](/wiki/SKY_Sport_(New_Zealand) "SKY Sport (New Zealand)").[[97]](#cite_note-99)[[98]](#cite_note-100)[[99]](#cite_note-101) Prime broadcasts select games live in New Zealand starting from the 2022 season, and will continue to broadcast every Warriors match on delay.

#### Detailed Australian coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Detailed Australian coverage")]

#### By Match-day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: By Match-day")]

* *Thursday Night Footy* is broadcast live on the [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") and simulcast on [Fox League](/wiki/Fox_League "Fox League") and [Kayo](/wiki/Kayo_Sports "Kayo Sports"). Kickoff is at 7:50 pm.
* *[Friday Night Football](/wiki/Friday_Night_Football_(NRL) "Friday Night Football (NRL)")* consists of 2 matches. The first match kicks off at 6:00 pm and is broadcast live on [Fox League](/wiki/Fox_League "Fox League"). The second match kicks off at 8:00 pm and is broadcast live on the [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") and simulcast on [Fox League](/wiki/Fox_League "Fox League") and [Kayo](/wiki/Kayo_Sports "Kayo Sports").
* *Super Saturday* consists of three matches. The first match kicks off at 3:00 pm, the second match kicks off at 5:30 pm and the third match kicks off at 7:35 pm. All three matches are broadcast live on [Fox League](/wiki/Fox_League "Fox League") and are simulcast live on [Kayo](/wiki/Kayo_Sports "Kayo Sports"). During the final five rounds of the year, the 7:35pm match is also simulcast on [Nine](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network").
* *NRL Sunday Ticket* consists of two matches. The first match kicks off at 2:00 pm and is broadcast live on [Fox League](/wiki/Fox_League "Fox League") and [Kayo](/wiki/Kayo_Sports "Kayo Sports"). The second match kicks off at 4:10 pm and is broadcast live on the [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") and simulcasted on [Fox League](/wiki/Fox_League "Fox League") and [Kayo](/wiki/Kayo_Sports "Kayo Sports"). During daylight savings, the 2:00 pm game is moved to 6:15 pm.

#### By Network

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: By Network")]

* [Fox League](/wiki/Fox_League "Fox League") and [Kayo](/wiki/Kayo_Sports "Kayo Sports") televise all matches live and commercial free (excluding the grand final).
* [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") televises the Thursday night game, the second Friday night game and the second Sunday afternoon game (first in daylight savings), as well as the last five Saturday 7:30pm games of the year. In addition to this, Nine also televise the annual [NRL Good Friday Game](/wiki/NRL_Good_Friday_Game "NRL Good Friday Game") and [Anzac Day Cup](/wiki/Anzac_Day_Cup "Anzac Day Cup") played at 4:05pm in addition to their allotted games for that round. In the finals series, Nine televise all games including the [NRL Grand Final](/wiki/NRL_Grand_Final "NRL Grand Final").
* The [State of Origin series](/wiki/State_of_Origin_series "State of Origin series") is televised exclusively live on the [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network"), with extensive pre-game and post-game coverage, starting at 7:00 pm.
* The [NRL Grand Final](/wiki/NRL_Grand_Final "NRL Grand Final") televised exclusively live on the [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network"). Since 2013, the match has kicked off at 7:30 pm

**Source:[[100]](#cite_note-102)**

### Internet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Internet")]

Outside of Australia, New Zealand, and the Pacific Islands, all matches are currently available in high definition on the subscription streaming service [WatchNRL](/wiki/WatchNRL "WatchNRL") which operates through its website and a dedicated app. Watch NRL is operated by Fox Sports Australia.

A selection of classic NRL games is available for free worldwide on the league's website.

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Radio")]

#### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Australia")]

The NRL has several exclusive rights partners to broadcast matches live via radio nationwide. FM coverage is provided by [Triple M](/wiki/Triple_M "Triple M") while AM coverage is contracted to [ABC Local Radio](/wiki/ABC_Local_Radio "ABC Local Radio") and [2GB](/wiki/2GB "2GB"). All radio coverage is available to be streamed live from the NRL website.

2GB has the commercial rights to four matches per week, covering the game through the [Continuous Call Team](/wiki/Continuous_Call_Team "Continuous Call Team") program.[[103]](#cite_note-samuelgordonstewart.com-105) 2GB also air representative games, as well as all matches throughout the finals series, and all three matches on Grand Final day. The coverage is networked to stations across the country, typically those owned by [Southern Cross Media Group](/wiki/Southern_Cross_Media_Group "Southern Cross Media Group"), [Grant Broadcasters](/wiki/Grant_Broadcasters "Grant Broadcasters") and other station groups on [Nine Radio](/wiki/Nine_Radio "Nine Radio").

ABC Local Radio has the rights to seven NRL matches per week in the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory"), [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") and [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales").

Triple M has exclusive access to Thursday night, Saturday 4 pm and Sunday night matches and is broadcast on commercial stations across the country.[[103]](#cite_note-samuelgordonstewart.com-105) Triple M also broadcast the State of Origin series, the finals series and the grand final.[[104]](#cite_note-106)[[105]](#cite_note-107)

[2SM](/wiki/2SM "2SM") formerly held the rights to air Thursday Night and Sunday 4pm games via NRL Nation. These games were also aired across their affiliates across NSW. [2UE](/wiki/2UE "2UE") also formerly held rights to matches.

**Current Broadcasters:**[[106]](#cite_note-108)[[107]](#cite_note-109)

* [Triple M](/wiki/Triple_M "Triple M"): Four games live per week (includes all Cowboys, Raiders, Knights and Titans home game live on home stations)
* [2GB](/wiki/2GB "2GB"): Four games live per week (includes Sunday 4:00pm exclusive)
* [ABC](/wiki/ABC_Radio_Grandstand "ABC Radio Grandstand"): Seven games live per week

### Print

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Rugby_League&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Print")]

[Big League](/wiki/Big_League "Big League") was the competition's official publication, released Thursday and produced by News Magazines. The publication ceased operations in 2020 leaving the game without an official program.[[108]](#cite_note-110) Another prominent magazine, *[Rugby League Week](/wiki/Rugby_League_Week "Rugby League Week")* ceased production in April 2017.

The only print magazine currently in circulation is the bi-monthly *[Rugby League Review](/wiki/Rugby_League_Review "Rugby League Review")*, which has been running since 2002. LeagueUnlimited's Front Row Magazine provides an unofficial match program each week in a digital magazine format.[[109]](#cite_note-111)

The Flames, the cheersquad for the St. George Illawarra Dragons, performing during an NRL match in 2018.
